elements.sent-2411-001: The proposed method significantly reduces the size of the dataset, preserving its essential manifold structure through an advanced spectral compression technique.
elements.sent-2411-002: This allows UMAP to perform much faster while maintaining the quality of its embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-003: Experiments on real-world datasets, such as USPS, demonstrate the method's ability to achieve substantial data reduction without compromising embedding fidelity.
elements.sent-2411-004: Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP) \cite{mcinnes2018umap has emerged as a powerful tool for dimensionality reduction and data visualization, particularly for uncovering complex manifold structures in high-dimensional data.}
elements.sent-2411-005: Unlike traditional methods such as PCA, which rely on linear transformations, UMAP preserves both local and global relationships by constructing a high-dimensional graph representation of the data and optimizing its embedding in a lower-dimensional space.
elements.sent-2411-006: This ability to maintain intricate relationships while providing high-quality visualizations has made UMAP a popular choice in fields ranging from bioinformatics to natural language processing.
elements.sent-2411-007: Despite its effectiveness, UMAP faces significant computational challenges when applied to large-scale datasets.
elements.sent-2411-008: Specifically, the graph construction and optimization steps of UMAP involve neighbor searches and iterative optimization, resulting in a time complexity of \(O(N \log N)\) for approximate nearest neighbor searches and up to \(O(N^2)\) for the full graph construction in dense data \cite{mcinnes2018umap .}
elements.sent-2411-009: This complexity becomes a bottleneck in big data scenarios, where the sheer volume of data points leads to a rapid increase in computation time.
elements.sent-2411-010: Additionally, UMAP’s performance can degrade in high-dimensional spaces due to the curse of dimensionality, where distances lose interpretability, further impacting both speed and embedding quality.
elements.sent-2411-011: These limitations restrict UMAP’s scalability and applicability in emerging data-intensive tasks, highlighting the need for an optimized approach to enhance its efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-012: Inspired by \cite{wang2023accelerate , in this paper, we propose a novel spectrum-preserving data compression method to address UMAP’s efficiency and scalability challenges.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Our approach first generates a significantly smaller, compressed dataset while preserving the essential manifold structure of the original data, utilizing a nearly-linear time spectral data compression technique.
elements.sent-2411-014: This compressed representation substantially reduces the computational burden of neighbor searches and graph construction, enabling UMAP to handle large-scale datasets more efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-015: The compressed data is then processed by the standard UMAP algorithm, preserving the quality of the low-dimensional embeddings without sacrificing interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-016: An additional advantage of the proposed method is its flexibility, allowing users to specify the desired compression ratio based on computational constraints or quality requirements.
elements.sent-2411-017: Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP) \cite{mcinnes2018umap is a state-of-the-art dimensionality reduction algorithm designed for both visualization and preserving the manifold structure of high-dimensional data.}
elements.sent-2411-018: UMAP builds upon concepts from manifold learning and topological data analysis, making it highly effective for uncovering the intrinsic geometry of complex datasets.
elements.sent-2411-019: It operates through two main phases: graph construction and low-dimensional embedding optimization.
elements.sent-2411-020: In the graph construction phase, UMAP builds a weighted \(k\)-nearest neighbor (k-NN) graph to represent the local neighborhood relationships in the high-dimensional space.
elements.sent-2411-021: For each data point \(\mathbf{x _i\), the algorithm identifies its \(k\) nearest neighbors based on a user-defined distance metric (e.g., Euclidean or cosine distance).}
elements.sent-2411-022: A local connectivity structure is then formed by assigning weights to edges in the graph, reflecting the probability that two points are connected based on their distance.
elements.sent-2411-023: This weighted graph preserves the local density and proximity relationships within the data.
elements.sent-2411-024: In the embedding optimization phase, UMAP optimizes a low-dimensional representation of the data that approximates the high-dimensional graph.
elements.sent-2411-025: The algorithm minimizes the divergence between the high-dimensional and low-dimensional representations by treating them as probabilistic graphs.
elements.sent-2411-026: Specifically, it applies stochastic gradient descent (SGD) to optimize the embedding, preserving both local and global structures.
elements.sent-2411-027: The result is a lower-dimensional projection of the data that retains key topological properties.
elements.sent-2411-028: Formally, the UMAP algorithm can be summarized in the following steps:.
elements.sent-2411-030: For each point \(\mathbf{x _i\), compute the \(k\)-nearest neighbors based on the chosen distance metric.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Construct a weighted \(k\)-NN graph with edge weights determined by local distance probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-032: Initialize a low-dimensional embedding of the data.
elements.sent-2411-033: Use stochastic gradient descent to minimize the difference between the high-dimensional and low-dimensional graph structures.
elements.sent-2411-034: However, UMAP’s computational efficiency can be a challenge for large-scale datasets.
elements.sent-2411-035: The graph construction phase involves computing pairwise distances and building a \(k\)-nearest neighbor graph, which has a worst-case complexity of \(\mathbf{O(N^2)}\).
elements.sent-2411-036: While approximate nearest neighbor methods reduce this complexity to \(\mathbf{O(N \log N)}\), the embedding optimization phase remains computationally intensive, requiring iterative updates that scale with the size of the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-037: These challenges highlight the need for methods to improve UMAP’s scalability.
elements.sent-2411-038: In particular, large datasets and high-dimensional spaces exacerbate the computational burden, motivating the development of techniques such as data compression to reduce the input size while preserving essential manifold properties.
elements.sent-2411-039: A graph \( G = (V, E, w) \) consists of a vertex set \( V \), an edge set \( E \), and a weight function \( w \) that assigns positive weights to the edges.
elements.sent-2411-040: The Laplacian matrix of \( G \), denoted as \( L_G \), is a symmetric diagonally dominant (SDD) matrix defined as: \begin{equation}\label{formula_laplacian} L_G(p, q) = \begin{cases} -w(p, q) \text{if } (p, q) \in E, \sum\limits_{(p, t) \in E} w(p, t) \text{if } p = q, 0 \text{otherwise}. \end{cases}\end{equation} In spectral graph theory \cite{chung1997spectral , the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the graph Laplacian encode valuable information about the graph’s structure, particularly its clustering properties.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{formula_laplacian} L_G(p, q) =  -w(p, q) if  (p, q) \in E, \sum\limits_{(p, t) \in E} w(p, t) if  p = q, 0 otherwise. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-041: By leveraging this spectral information, one can uncover latent relationships in the data that are not evident in the original feature space.
elements.sent-2411-042: For instance, in synthetic datasets such as two-moons and two-circles, applying $k$-means directly in the original feature space often leads to incorrect clustering due to the algorithm's dependence on Euclidean distance.
elements.sent-2411-043: However, transforming the data into the spectral embedding space using the graph Laplacian aligns the feature space with the intrinsic geometry of the data, enabling $k$-means to accurately identify clusters and reflect the true underlying structure.
elements.sent-2411-044: Given a dataset of samples, we first construct a $k$-nearest neighbors (k-NN) graph \( G \) to capture the underlying manifold structure of the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-045: From \( G \), the corresponding graph Laplacian matrix \( L_G \) is derived.
elements.sent-2411-046: The spectral similarity between data points is computed using the following procedure:.
elements.sent-2411-047: Extract the bottom \( K \) nontrivial eigenvectors of the graph Laplacian;.
elements.sent-2411-048: Organize these eigenvectors as column vectors to form a matrix \( U \);.
elements.sent-2411-049: Represent each data point as a low-dimensional feature vector corresponding to a row of \( U \);.
elements.sent-2411-050: Define the spectral similarity \( s_{uv \) between two points \( u \) and \( v \) as follows \cite{livne2012lean :.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Here, \( \mathbf{x _u \) and \( \mathbf{x _v \) represent the feature vectors of points \( u \) and \( v \) in the spectral embedding space.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Statistically, \( s_{uv \) quantifies the proportion of variance explained by the linear relationship between \( \mathbf{x _u \) and \( \mathbf{x _v \) in the spectral domain.}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: A larger \( s_{uv \) value indicates a stronger spectral connection between the points.}
elements.sent-2411-054: To reduce the computational cost of eigen-decomposition, Gauss-Seidel relaxation can be used to generate smoothed embedding vectors, achieving linear time complexity \cite{livne2012lean .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Using spectral similarity, the dataset is partitioned into subsets where points within the same subset exhibit high spectral correlation.
elements.sent-2411-056: For each subset, the mean of its feature vectors is computed to produce a spectrally representative pseudo-sample.
elements.sent-2411-057: These pseudo-samples collectively form a compressed version of the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-058: If further compression is required, this process can be repeated iteratively.
elements.sent-2411-059: Fig.~\ref{fig:USPS7NN and Fig.~\ref{fig:USPS2X compare the \( k \)-NN graphs of the original and compressed USPS datasets projected into 2D, illustrating that the compressed dataset successfully retains the manifold structure of the original data.}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[scale=0.2]{USPS2NN.png} \caption{The \( k \)-NN graph of the original USPS dataset.\protect\label{fig:USPS7NN}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[scale=0.2]{USPS2X.png} \caption{The \( k \)-NN graph of the compressed USPS dataset.\protect\label{fig:USPS2X}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Then, the UMAP process can be applied on the compressed data points to generate a low-dimensional embedding that preserves the manifold structure of the original dataset.
elements.sent-2411-061: Input: A dataset $D$ with $N$ samples $x_1, \dots, x_N \in \mathbb{R ^d$.}
elements.algorithmic-2411-001: [1] \STATE Construct a $k$-nearest neighbor ($k$-NN) graph $G$ to model the local manifold structure of the data. \STATE Derive the Laplacian matrix $L_G$ from the $k$-NN graph $G$. \STATE Apply spectrum-preserving data compression to reduce the dataset size while retaining manifold properties. \STATE Use UMAP on the compressed dataset to generate a low-dimensional embedding.
elements.sent-2411-063: The spectrum-preserving data compression requires $O(|E_G|\log(|V|))$ time, where $|E_G|$ denotes the number of edges in the original graph, and $|V|$ represents the number of data points.
elements.sent-2411-064: Applying UMAP on the compressed dataset has a complexity of $O(P\log(P))$, where $P$ is the number of compressed points, reflecting the approximate nearest neighbor search and optimization steps.
elements.sent-2411-065: We use USPS data set to evaluate the method.
elements.sent-2411-066: The UMAP visualization results of the original USPS dataset and the dataset compressed by a factor of five are shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:USPS7NN and Fig.~\ref{fig:USPS7NN , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: As observed, even after a five-fold compression, the compressed dataset effectively preserves the structure of the ten clusters in USPS, demonstrating its ability to maintain the underlying clustering characteristics.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[scale=0.2]{uspsumaporig.png} \caption{UMAP Visualization of the Original USPS Dataset.\protect\label{fig:USPS7NN}}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[scale=0.2]{umap5Xusps.png} \caption{UMAP Visualization of the USPS Dataset Compressed by a Factor of Five.\protect\label{fig:USPS2X}}
elements.sent-2411-068: To address the computational challenges of UMAP, this study introduces a novel spectral data compression approach that generates a substantially smaller dataset while retaining the essential spectral properties necessary for manifold preservation.
elements.sent-2411-069: Experiments on large-scale, real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed method significantly accelerates the UMAP algorithm without compromising embedding quality.
